questions.question: When was the Micius satellite launched?
questions.explanation: Micius was launched on August 16, 2016, from the Jiuquan Satellite Launch Center in China, becoming the world's first quantum science satellite.

questions.question: What was the longest entanglement distribution distance achieved by Micius?
questions.explanation: Micius distributed entangled photon pairs between two ground stations (Delingha and Lijiang) separated by 1,203 km, achieving the longest entanglement distribution ever demonstrated.

questions.question: What Bell inequality value did Micius measure for the 1,203 km entanglement?
questions.explanation: Micius measured S = 2.37 ± 0.09, which exceeds the classical limit of 2 and confirms quantum entanglement was successfully distributed over 1,203 km. The maximum quantum value is 2√2 ≈ 2.83.

questions.question: How did Micius achieve intercontinental QKD between China and Austria?
questions.explanation: Micius acted as a trusted relay, establishing separate QKD links with ground stations in China and Austria. The satellite combined the keys to enable secure communication over 7,600 km.

questions.question: What was Micius's QKD key rate at 1,200 km distance?
questions.explanation: Micius achieved approximately 1 kbps secure key rate at 1,200 km. While modest, this is approximately 10 million times higher than what would be theoretically possible with fiber at the same distance.

questions.question: What is a current limitation of Micius-style satellite QKD?

options.text: Can only work at specific times of year
Limited to nighttime operation due to background noise

questions.explanation: Micius is currently limited to nighttime operation because sunlight creates too much background noise for the single-photon detectors. Future systems aim to achieve daytime operation through better filtering and timing.

questions.question: Micius was the first demonstration of quantum teleportation from ground to space.
questions.explanation: True. In 2017, Micius demonstrated the first ground-to-satellite quantum teleportation over distances of 500-1,400 km, achieving fidelities of about 80%, well above the classical limit.
